_title: IEC 60364-5-52 Annex G — Voltage Drop in Consumers' Installations
_basis: IEC 60364-5-52:2009+AMD1:2017 Annex G (informative) and IEC 60364-1 Clause 12.6
_note: Annex G is INFORMATIVE — the limits are recommendations, not mandatory requirements. However, most national implementations (BS 7671, NF C 15-100, etc.) adopt these limits as mandatory. Always check the applicable national code for mandatory limits. For projects in IEC jurisdictions without a specific national code, apply the Annex G limits as good engineering practice.

voltage_drop_limits._measurement: From the origin of the consumer's installation (service entrance) to the furthest point of use

lighting_circuits._comment: Stricter limit for lighting because voltage variations cause perceptible flicker and affect light output (lumen depreciation)

where_supply_agrees._note: By agreement with the supply authority, higher voltage drops may be permitted for specific long circuits (rural supplies, large industrial premises). This must be documented and agreed in writing.
where_supply_agrees.higher_limit_example_agreed: Up to 8% where documented, for very long circuits (> 200m) in industrial premises

origin_definition.note: The 'origin of the installation' is the point of delivery from the supply network — the service termination, metering position, or main switch. Voltage drop IN the supply network (service cable from transformer to meter) is not included — that is the supply authority's responsibility.
origin_definition.practical_start_point: Main Distribution Board (MDB) or Consumer Unit (CU)

_where.mV_A_m: Voltage drop rate from cable rating tables (2-conductor loop value from part5-52-cable-ratings-copper.json)
_where.Ib: Design current in amperes (load current, not device rating)
_where.L: One-way circuit length in metres (NOT double — the mV/A/m value already accounts for both conductors)

three_phase_balanced._note: Option 2 converts the single-phase 2-conductor mV/A/m to three-phase. Factor 0.866 = √3/2. This gives line-to-neutral voltage drop.

resistive_vs_reactive.note_for_large_cables: For cables ≥ 50mm², cable reactance (X ≈ 0.07–0.10 mΩ/m per conductor) contributes to voltage drop, particularly for inductive loads (power factor < 1). The formula above uses resistive mV/A/m only. For large cables at low power factor, add the reactive component: ΔU = Ib × L × (R cosφ + X sinφ) / 1000.

mV_per_A_per_m_reference_table._title: Resistive mV/A/m values for copper conductors at operating temperature (two-conductor loop, single-phase)
mV_per_A_per_m_reference_table._basis: Conductor resistance at maximum operating temperature (70°C for PVC, 90°C for XLPE). For three-phase, multiply by 0.866.
mV_per_A_per_m_reference_table._note: Values in cable rating tables (part5-52-cable-ratings-copper.json) are the same. Reproduced here for quick reference.
mV_per_A_per_m_reference_table._three_phase_factor: Multiply values above by 0.866 for balanced three-phase circuits

maximum_circuit_lengths_reference._title: Approximate maximum circuit length (metres) before 3% voltage drop is exceeded — at full rated current
maximum_circuit_lengths_reference._basis: L_max = (limit_V × 1000) / (mV/A/m × In), where limit = 6.9V for lighting (3%), 11.5V for power (5%)
maximum_circuit_lengths_reference._note: These are maximums at full device rating. Actual circuits often run at less than rated current — actual length can be longer. Use for quick feasibility checks only.

example_1_lighting_circuit._title: Check voltage drop on 1.5mm² lighting circuit, 12A load, 35m run
example_1_lighting_circuit.action: Increase to 2.5mm² (mV/A/m = 18): VD = (18 × 12 × 35) / 1000 = 7.56V (3.3%) — still marginal. Use 4mm² (mV/A/m = 11): VD = (11 × 12 × 35) / 1000 = 4.62V (2.0%) — PASSES.

example_2_three_phase_submain._title: Three-phase 95mm² XLPE submain, 200A load, 80m to sub-distribution board

example_3_long_rural_circuit._title: Rural single-phase circuit, 16A MCB, 6mm² cable, 80m to outbuilding
example_3_long_rural_circuit.note: For a lighting circuit this would fail (3% limit). Ensure the outbuilding lighting is fed from a separate lighting circuit, or use 10mm² for the combined run.

voltage_drop_at_motor_start._note: Motor starting current is typically 6–8× full load current. The voltage dip during starting is NOT a voltage drop check under Annex G — it is a transient that must be acceptable to other loads on the same circuit. Starting voltage dip should be limited to < 5% at motor terminals (where other sensitive loads are present). Calculate: ΔU_start = I_start × (R1 + R2) where R1 + R2 is the source impedance.
voltage_drop_at_motor_start.impact_on_nearby_loads: Voltage dips > 5% at supply terminals can cause lamp flicker, computer resets, and relay dropout. Where sensitive loads coexist with motors, use soft starters or VFDs to reduce starting current.
